citation: Biology 206: DNA Structure & Replication (Chapter 9), Gene Action & Expression (Chapters 10 & 11), Gene Mutation & Chromosomes (Chapters 12 & 13), Genomics (Chapter 14) - McGraw Hill

questions.question: During DNA replication, why does DNA polymerase require an RNA primer synthesized by primase rather than initiating synthesis directly?
options: a- DNA polymerase can add nucleotides to any 5' end without a starting point
b- RNA primers increase the speed of DNA synthesis by 50%
c- DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides to a free 3' OH group; primase provides this required group
d- Primers are needed to prevent mutation hotspots from forming
e- The 3' OH group from RNA primers acts as a marker for ligase
questions.answer: c- DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides to a free 3' OH group; primase provides this required group

questions.question: What is the consequence of the antiparallel orientation of DNA strands during replication?
options: a- Both strands are synthesized continuously toward the replication fork
b- The leading strand is synthesized continuously while the lagging strand requires Okazaki fragments
c- The 5' to 3' directionality becomes irrelevant during replication
d- RNA primers are only needed on the leading strand
e- Ligase activity doubles on the leading strand
questions.answer: b- The leading strand is synthesized continuously while the lagging strand requires Okazaki fragments

questions.question: In Sanger sequencing, how do ddNTPs (dideoxynucleotides) differ functionally from regular dNTPs?
options: a- ddNTPs are fluorescently labeled while dNTPs are not
b- ddNTPs lack a 3' OH group, causing chain termination when incorporated
c- ddNTPs have extra hydrogen bonds to the template strand
d- ddNTPs are only used in the annealing phase of PCR
e- ddNTPs increase DNA polymerase speed significantly
questions.answer: b- ddNTPs lack a 3' OH group, causing chain termination when incorporated

questions.question: Why is Taq polymerase essential for PCR amplification cycles?
options: a- Taq polymerase binds more tightly to DNA templates than standard polymerases
b- Taq polymerase is thermostable; it maintains function during repeated 95°C heating cycles
c- Taq polymerase produces fluorescent nucleotides needed for visualization
d- Taq polymerase removes RNA primers faster than other polymerases
e- Taq polymerase can synthesize DNA in both 5' to 3' and 3' to 5' directions
questions.answer: b- Taq polymerase is thermostable; it maintains function during repeated 95°C heating cycles

questions.question: What is the primary molecular difference between germline and somatic mutations?
options: a- Germline mutations only affect liver cells while somatic mutations affect all tissues
b- Germline mutations occur during meiosis and are inherited; somatic mutations occur during mitosis and affect only some body cells
c- Somatic mutations are always more severe than germline mutations
d- Germline mutations cannot cause cancer while somatic mutations always do
e- Germline mutations repair themselves while somatic mutations do not
questions.answer: b- Germline mutations occur during meiosis and are inherited; somatic mutations occur during mitosis and affect only some body cells

questions.question: How do mutational hotspots involving short repetitive sequences (like AAAAAA...) cause mutations?
options: a- Repetitive sequences attract mutagenic chemicals from the environment
b- DNA polymerase dissociates and reanneals incorrectly at repeats, creating loops that result in indels
c- Repetitive sequences prevent RNA primers from binding properly
d- Short repeats interfere with transcription factor binding
e- Repetitive sequences block proteasomal degradation pathways
questions.answer: b- DNA polymerase dissociates and reanneals incorrectly at repeats, creating loops that result in indels

questions.question: Why does a frameshift mutation typically have more severe phenotypic consequences than a missense mutation?
options: a- Frameshift mutations always cause premature cell death
b- Frameshift mutations change only one amino acid while missense affects multiple
c- Frameshift mutations shift the reading frame, altering nearly ALL downstream amino acids; missense changes only one
d- Frameshift mutations prevent transcription entirely
e- Missense mutations activate protective genes while frameshift mutations don't
questions.answer: c- Frameshift mutations shift the reading frame, altering nearly ALL downstream amino acids; missense changes only one

questions.question: What does 'anticipation' mean in the context of repeat expansion disorders like Huntington disease?
options: a- The disease symptoms appear before other genetic markers
b- Trinucleotide repeats expand with each generation, causing earlier onset and more severe phenotype
c- Patients anticipate their disease will worsen over time
d- The disorder can be predicted by parental genotype alone
e- Anticipation refers to preventive medical treatment before symptoms appear
questions.answer: b- Trinucleotide repeats expand with each generation, causing earlier onset and more severe phenotype

questions.question: Why are autosomal trisomies (like Down syndrome) more likely to result in live births than autosomal monosomies?
options: a- Trisomies are always less severe than monosomies genetically
b- Loss of genetic material (monosomy) causes more severe gene dosage imbalance than gain (trisomy)
c- Trisomy 21 contains genes that prevent miscarriage
d- Monosomies can only occur in males while trisomies occur in females
e- The placenta cannot support monosomy pregnancies
questions.answer: b- Loss of genetic material (monosomy) causes more severe gene dosage imbalance than gain (trisomy)

questions.question: How do transcription factors 'link the genome to the environment' during gene expression?
options: a- Transcription factors physically transport DNA outside the cell
b- External signals activate transcription factors that bind TATA boxes and recruit RNA polymerase to initiate transcription
c- Environmental toxins directly mutate transcription factor genes
d- Transcription factors convert mRNA into proteins in response to stress
e- The environment directly modifies DNA base pairing
questions.answer: b- External signals activate transcription factors that bind TATA boxes and recruit RNA polymerase to initiate transcription

questions.question: How does alternative splicing allow ~20,325 human genes to produce over 1 million different proteins?
options: a- Alternative splicing creates new genes by recombining exons from different chromosomes
b- Each gene undergoes alternative splicing only once, producing different proteins in different tissues
c- 95% of human genes use alternative splicing; different exon combinations produce protein variants from the same gene
d- Alternative splicing adds extra nucleotides to mRNA, extending protein length
e- Alternative splicing allows genes to encode multiple start codons
questions.answer: c- 95% of human genes use alternative splicing; different exon combinations produce protein variants from the same gene

questions.question: How can microRNA (miRNA) abundance be manipulated therapeutically in cancer treatment?
options: a- Cancer cells cannot be affected by miRNA changes
b- Increasing all miRNA levels will always kill cancer cells
c- Cancer cells have abnormal miRNA levels; drugs can reduce miRNAs that silence tumor suppressors or increase miRNAs that block oncogenes
d- miRNAs are only present in normal cells, not cancer cells
e- Drugs can permanently eliminate all miRNAs from the body
questions.answer: c- Cancer cells have abnormal miRNA levels; drugs can reduce miRNAs that silence tumor suppressors or increase miRNAs that block oncogenes

questions.question: Why must eukaryotic mRNA be processed with a 5' cap and 3' poly-A tail before translation?
options: a- The cap and tail are decorative modifications with no functional purpose
b- 5' cap is a ribosome recognition site; 3' poly-A tail stabilizes short-lived mRNA and facilitates nucleus exit
c- Caps and tails prevent introns from being removed
d- Processing increases the speed of transcription by 10-fold
e- Only prokaryotic mRNA requires these modifications
questions.answer: b- 5' cap is a ribosome recognition site; 3' poly-A tail stabilizes short-lived mRNA and facilitates nucleus exit

questions.question: How does the proteasome prevent accumulation of misfolded proteins that would cause cellular disease?
options: a- The proteasome transports misfolded proteins to other cells
b- Misfolded proteins tag themselves for proteasomal degradation
c- The proteasome tunnel straightens misfolded proteins and cuts them into amino acids for reuse
d- The proteasome prevents all proteins from folding
e- Misfolded proteins are expelled from the cell into the bloodstream
questions.answer: c- The proteasome tunnel straightens misfolded proteins and cuts them into amino acids for reuse

questions.question: During translation elongation, how does the ribosome advance one codon down the mRNA?
options: a- The ribosome removes the entire P site tRNA and brings in a new one
b- tRNA in A site forms peptide bond; ribosome translocates moving P site tRNA out, A site tRNA to P site, new tRNA enters empty A site
c- The ribosome spins around the mRNA to read the next codon
d- New tRNAs are synthesized for each codon position
e- The mRNA moves through the ribosome while tRNAs remain stationary
questions.answer: b- tRNA in A site forms peptide bond; ribosome translocates moving P site tRNA out, A site tRNA to P site, new tRNA enters empty A site

questions.question: What structural and functional differences distinguish euchromatin from heterochromatin?
options: a- Euchromatin is condensed and transcriptionally silent; heterochromatin is loose and active
b- Euchromatin is loosely packed (~2% open/readable) and transcriptionally active; heterochromatin is condensed and silent found at telomeres/centromeres
c- Both types are equally accessible to transcription factors
d- Heterochromatin contains more genes than euchromatin
e- Euchromatin can never convert to heterochromatin
questions.answer: b- Euchromatin is loosely packed (~2% open/readable) and transcriptionally active; heterochromatin is condensed and silent found at telomeres/centromeres

questions.question: How do methyl groups added to histones act as epigenetic silencers of gene expression?
options: a- Methyl groups block all transcription in the cell
b- Methyl groups cause DNA polymerase to stop working
c- Methyl groups keep DNA in heterochromatic (closed) configuration, preventing transcription factor access to promoters
d- Methyl groups increase RNA polymerase activity
e- Methyl groups replace histone proteins entirely
questions.answer: c- Methyl groups keep DNA in heterochromatic (closed) configuration, preventing transcription factor access to promoters

questions.question: What is the consequence of a nonsense mutation that converts an amino acid codon to a stop codon?
options: a- The amino acid is replaced with a similar amino acid
b- Translation continues normally with an extra stop signal
c- A truncated nonfunctional protein is produced due to premature termination
d- The mutation has no effect on protein synthesis
e- The stop codon reverses back to the original amino acid
questions.answer: c- A truncated nonfunctional protein is produced due to premature termination

questions.question: How can a karyotype and cfDNA testing be used together for chromosomal abnormality detection in pregnancy?
options: a- cfDNA is more invasive than karyotypes so it should be done first
b- cfDNA testing is noninvasive (blood test) detecting aneuploidies; abnormal cfDNA results prompt confirmatory karyotyping or ultrasound
c- Karyotypes can only be done after birth, cfDNA cannot detect any abnormalities
d- Both tests are equally invasive and carry equal miscarriage risk
e- Karyotypes and cfDNA detect completely different types of mutations
questions.answer: b- cfDNA testing is noninvasive (blood test) detecting aneuploidies; abnormal cfDNA results prompt confirmatory karyotyping or ultrasound
